Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have an organized study routine at all. I'd go through phases where I wouldn't touch anything for days, then suddenly cram everything when anxiety hit me hard. Most of my studying happened in intense bursts, usually late at night when the pressure became unbearable. I know it's chaotic, but somehow my brain just works better under that kind of stress, even though it wrecks me emotionally.
Q2: My pattern was completely dictated by deadlines and my mental state at any given moment. Some weeks I'd barely open my laptop, other weeks I'd be studying 24 hours straight before an exam. Regular schedules feel suffocating to me, and I've never been able to stick to one. When I'm feeling anxious, I either avoid everything or hyperfocus—there's no in-between.
Q3: Usually panic. Like, I'd realize an exam was coming up or I'd get a notification about something due, and that fear would finally push me to log in. Sometimes boredom would make me check in just to see what I was missing, but that usually made me feel worse. The gamified tasks they introduced actually helped sometimes because earning points felt less threatening than facing a wall of assignments.
Q4: Not really, no. The LMS never became a habit for me—it was always something I approached with dread. The only time it felt somewhat routine was during exam periods when I had no choice but to log in repeatedly. Even then, it felt more like a crisis response than an actual routine.
Q5: The video lectures were the most useful because I'm a visual learner and I could actually see concepts being explained. I'd often watch them at 1.5x speed during my cramming sessions. The gamified tasks were surprisingly helpful too—they broke things into smaller chunks that felt less overwhelming. Anything with visual diagrams or demonstrations stuck with me better than reading walls of text.
Q6: I basically ignored the discussion forums and most of the reading materials. The discussions felt pointless to me—just people posting to meet participation requirements without saying anything meaningful. Reading assignments were boring and I'd zone out immediately, so I just skipped them and relied on videos instead. I know I probably missed important stuff, but I couldn't force myself to care.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to exam content or if it had some kind of immediate payoff, like points or badges. The gamified elements actually worked on me even though I knew they were kind of manipulative. If something felt abstract or disconnected from what I'd be tested on, I couldn't justify spending energy on it when I was already struggling to keep up.
Q8: Required activities felt like chains around my neck, honestly. I'd put them off until the absolute last second because the pressure made me freeze up. Optional activities were easier to ignore completely, but sometimes I'd do them if they seemed interesting or had game-like elements. The irony is I probably would have engaged more if everything was optional—the requirement aspect triggers my avoidance.
Q9: Yeah, a lot of my actual studying happened offline. I'd download lecture slides and videos, then study from my tablet without internet to avoid distractions. I also used YouTube videos from other professors explaining the same concepts, and sometimes those were better than our course materials. My notes were all handwritten because that helps me remember things visually.
Q10: Most of my exam prep wouldn't show up in the LMS at all. I'd print out slides and cover my desk with them, drawing connections and making visual maps. I also studied with one friend from another section—we'd quiz each other over Discord, which obviously doesn't get tracked. The night before exams, I'd be studying for 8-10 hours straight from printed materials.
Q11: Definitely. I'd watch a video once on the LMS, download the slides, and never go back to the platform. My actual learning happened with those downloaded files spread across my floor while I tried to make sense of everything. The LMS would show I accessed something once, but I might have reviewed those same slides twenty times offline.
Q12: The middle of the semester was brutal. My anxiety peaked, I was sleeping terribly, and everything felt pointless. I'd log into the LMS, see how behind I was, and immediately close it because facing that reality was too much. There were two weeks where I basically disappeared from the course entirely—just couldn't make myself engage with anything academic.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was exhausted or anxious, the LMS became this symbol of everything I was failing at. I'd avoid it entirely or just mindlessly click through things without absorbing anything. Competing responsibilities weren't really my issue—it was more that my mental state made everything feel impossible. The workload itself wasn't unreasonable, but my ability to handle it fluctuated wildly.
Q14: All the time. I'd have the LMS open while scrolling my phone, technically 'watching' a lecture but not processing a single word. Sometimes I'd click through readings just to mark them complete, knowing I'd have to actually learn the material later during my panic-cramming sessions. My engagement was often performative rather than meaningful.
Q15: I postponed almost every assignment until it was nearly too late. The anxiety around starting was worse than the anxiety around failing, somehow. I'd know something was important, open the instructions, feel overwhelmed, and close it immediately. This happened repeatedly until the deadline forced my hand. It's not laziness—it's like my brain physically won't let me start.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that made me use the LMS. Without them, I probably wouldn't have engaged at all. My usage would spike dramatically in the 24-48 hours before anything was due. It's unhealthy and I know it, but that deadline pressure is the only thing that cuts through my avoidance and anxiety enough to actually do work.
Q17: Feedback was a mixed bag. Good feedback on exams motivated me and proved my cramming strategy could work. But feedback on assignments I'd rushed often made me feel worse and more avoidant. I'd sometimes delay looking at feedback for days because I was scared of what it would say. When it was positive, though, it gave me a temporary boost.
Q18: The course structure felt rigid in ways that didn't work for my brain. Weekly modules with consistent deadlines sound good in theory, but they just gave me more things to avoid. The gamified tasks were a bright spot—they made engagement feel less threatening. But overall, the structure assumed a consistency I'm not capable of maintaining.
Q19: The gamification elements definitely made engagement easier—turning tasks into smaller, point-based activities reduced my anxiety about starting. What made it harder was the sheer volume of required readings and the discussion post requirements. Seeing a long list of incomplete tasks was paralyzing. A more visual progress tracker might have helped me feel less overwhelmed.
Q20: That's literally me. I'd log in, click around anxiously, watch bits of videos, check deadlines, panic about assignments, then close everything without actually submitting work. High activity doesn't mean productive activity—sometimes it's just anxiety manifesting as aimless clicking. The LMS became a place I'd visit to torture myself with reminders of what I wasn't doing.
Q21: This is also me, weirdly. My exam scores were good because I'm actually smart when I finally sit down and learn something. I just learn better in intense bursts with offline materials than through steady LMS engagement. Low activity can mean someone found more efficient ways to study, or like me, they crammed everything into invisible offline sessions.
Q22: Course logs can show when someone logs in and what they click, but they can't show understanding or actual learning. They miss all offline studying, all the mental processing that happens away from screens, and they definitely can't measure the quality of engagement. Someone clicking through everything might be learning nothing while someone who barely logs in might be mastering the material elsewhere.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about and connecting with the material—having it occupy your thoughts even when you're not actively studying. For me, engagement happens in bursts when I finally break through my avoidance. It's not about hours logged or tasks completed; it's about those moments when concepts click and I genuinely understand something. My engagement is real but chaotic.
Q24: The LMS can't show how much I actually learned during my offline cramming sessions, or how my anxiety shaped every interaction I had with the course. It can't capture the hours I spent with printed slides, the visual maps I drew, or the genuine understanding I developed before exams. It also can't show how much I struggled mentally just to open the platform, or how the gamified elements were sometimes the only thing that made engagement possible.
